commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=ec93971fb8d2cd8d36340ce397e669d5571b6124)
commands.review_summary:
The branch adds `MICROSOFT_TRACKER.md`, a GitHub-querying `scripts/generate-microsoft-tracker.mjs` generator with an optional PR-body update mode, and one `CHANGELOG.md` entry.

PR surface: Docs +423, Other +428. Total +851 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the patch defects: source inspection shows raw tracked titles are copied into generated markdown, and current generated output contains closing-keyword titles before the script writes the markdown to a PR body. I did not run the mutating path because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Manual review is needed because the draft protected PR needs maintainer direction on committed tracker automation versus PR-body-only metadata, plus proof and conflict resolution.

target.title: fix(markdown): a fenced-code line with trailing text is content, not a closing fence
target.body:
## Summary

Salvages #96687 by @ly-wang19 onto current `main` — clean single-commit rebase, original authorship preserved. The original was auto-closed by `openclaw-barnacle` for the author's >20-active-PR cap, not on merits; the bug is still live on `main`.

Fenced-code-block span scanning (used to keep chunk breaks out of code blocks) treats a marker line that carries trailing text (e.g. ```` ``` not a close ````) as a *closing* fence. Per CommonMark that line is code content and the block stays open — so the scanner ends the block early and can report an interior code offset as a "safe break," letting a chunker split inside the code block.

## Root Cause

**Symptom** — For `` ```\ncode\n``` not a close\nmore code\n```\n``, `parseFenceSpans` returns the block closed at the `` ``` not a close `` line, and `isSafeFenceBreak` returns `true` for an offset inside the still-open block.

**Root cause** — In `packages/markdown-core/src/fences.ts`, `scanFenceSpans()` closes a span when `open.markerChar === markerChar && markerLen >= open.markerLen`, but does not check that the rest of the marker line is blank. CommonMark §4.5: a closing fence "may be followed only by spaces or tabs." A marker followed by non-whitespace is an info-string-bearing line / content, not a close.

**Evidence** — On current `main` (line 61): `} else if (open.markerChar === markerChar && markerLen >= open.markerLen) {` with no trailing-text guard. The new regression test fails against this `main` source (see Real behavior proof).

**Fix + why this level** — Add `&& match[3].trim() === ""` to the close condition so only a bare/whitespace-trailed fence closes the block. This is the correct layer: the close-detection predicate is exactly where CommonMark's "trailing whitespace only" rule belongs; downstream consumers (`isSafeFenceBreak`) correctly derive from accurate spans once the span boundary is right.

**Scope / risk** — Touches only the closing-fence branch. Still closes on a bare fence, a longer same-marker fence, and preserves an opener info string (`` ```python ``) — all asserted in the test. Opener detection is unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

- **Regression test (new):** `packages/markdown-core/src/fences.test.ts` → "treats a marker line with trailing text as code content, not a closing fence" — **fails without the fix**.
- **Captured run on this branch:**

WITH fix:
```
 ✓ packages/markdown-core/src/fences.test.ts (2 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed (2)
```

WITHOUT fix (test against current `main` source for `fences.ts`):
```
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  1 failed | 1 passed (2)
```

## Changes from original

- Rebased onto current `main` (clean single commit, no conflicts).
- No code changes from @ly-wang19's original.

Credit: **Salvage of #96687 by @ly-wang19** — rebased onto current main. Original closed by the >20-active-PR queue cap, not on merits.
